long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Questions involving multiple reaction steps where each step's product becomes the reactant for the next

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions asking 'how many' compounds satisfy a given property, or enumeration of specific cases

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms beyond surface-level recall - intermediates, transition states, electron flow

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties like acidity, basicity, electrophilicity, nucleophilicity, stability, bond strength, etc.

questions.question_text: The major product formed in the following reaction is [Reaction scheme image]. Na in liquid NH3 reduces non terminal alkyne into trans alkene.
questions.solution_summary: Na in liquid NH3 reduces non-terminal alkyne into trans alkene.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of alkyne reduction with Na/NH3.

questions.question_text: Among the following, the conformation that corresponds to the most stable conformation of meso-butane-2,3-diol is [Image options]
questions.solution_summary: In option (B), the configuration represents meso-butane-2,3-diol and due to intramolecular hydrogen bonding, the gauche form is more stable. Options (C) and (D) do not represent meso-isomer.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of meso compounds and conformational stability.

questions.question_text: For the given close packed structure of a salt made of cation X and anion Y shown below (ions of only one face are shown for clarity), the packing fraction is approximately [Image]. Options: (A) 0.74 (B) 0.63 (C) 0.52 (D) 0.48
questions.solution_summary: Packing fraction = (3×(4/3)πr₁³ + 1×(4/3)πr₂³)/a³. For octahedral voids, r₂/r₁ = 0.414. 2r₁ = a. Substituting gives f = [1×(4/3)π{3×(0.414)³ + 1}]/2³ = 0.634 ≈ 0.63.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires calculation of packing fraction in a close packed structure.

questions.question_text: The calculated spin only magnetic moments of [Cr(NH3)6]3+ and [CuF6]3− in BM, respectively, are (Atomic number of Cr and Cu are 24 and 29, respectively). Options: (A) 3.87 and 2.84 (B) 4.90 and 1.73 (C) 3.87 and 1.73 (D) 4.90 and 2.84
questions.solution_summary: [Cr(NH3)6]3+ has Cr3+ (d3), NH3 strong field gives t2g3 with n=3 unpaired electrons; μ = √(3(3+2)) = 3.87 BM. [CuF6]3− has Cu3+ (d8), F− weak field gives high spin with n=2 unpaired electrons; μ = √(2(2+2)) = 2.84 BM.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of crystal field theory and magnetic moment calculation.

questions.question_text: For the following reaction scheme, percentage yields are given along the arrow: [Reaction scheme image]. X g and y g are mass of R and U, respectively. (Use: Molar mass H=1, C=12, O=16). The value of x is
questions.solution_summary: From the reaction scheme and yield calculations, the mass of R (x) is 1.62 g.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires multi-step yield calculation and molecular weight determination.

questions.question_text: For the following reaction scheme, percentage yields are given along the arrow: [Reaction scheme image]. X g and y g are mass of R and U, respectively. (Use: Molar mass H=1, C=12, O=16). The value of y is
questions.solution_summary: From the reaction scheme and yield calculations, the mass of U (y) is 0.80 g.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires multi-step yield calculation and molecular weight determination.

questions.question_text: For the reaction, X(s) ⇌ Y(s) + Z(g), the plot of ln(pZ/p°) versus 10^4/T is given below (in solid line), where pZ is the pressure (in bar) of the gas Z at temperature T and p° = 1 bar. Given, d(ln K)/d(1/T) = -ΔH°/R, where K = pZ/p° and R = 8.314 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. The value of standard enthalpy, ΔH° (in kJ mol⁻¹) for the given reaction is
questions.sub_topic: Van't Hoff equation, Enthalpy from ln K vs 1/T plot
questions.solution_summary: Slope of ln(pZ/p°) vs 10^4/T = -ΔH°/(R) * 10^4? Actually from the graph, slope = -(ΔH°)/R * 10^4? The solution gives ΔH° = 2 × 8.314 × 10^4 = 166.28 kJ/mol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires interpretation of Van't Hoff plot and enthalpy calculation.

questions.question_text: For the reaction, X(s) ⇌ Y(s) + Z(g), the plot of ln(pZ/p°) versus 10^4/T is given below (in solid line). The value of standard entropy change, ΔS° (in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹) is
questions.sub_topic: Van't Hoff equation, Entropy from intercept
questions.solution_summary: Using the intercept from the graph, ΔS° = 17 × 8.314 = 141.34 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires interpretation of Van't Hoff plot for entropy.

questions.question_text: The boiling point of water in a 0.1 molal silver nitrate solution (solution A) is x °C. To this solution A, an equal volume of 0.1 molal aqueous barium chloride solution is added to make a new solution B. The difference in the boiling points of water in the two solutions A and B is y × 10⁻² °C. (Assume: Densities of the solutions A and B are the same as that of water and the soluble salts dissociate completely. Use: Molal elevation constant Kb = 0.5 K kg mol⁻¹; Boiling point of pure water as 100°C.) The value of x is
questions.sub_topic: Colligative properties, Elevation of boiling point
questions.solution_summary: AgNO3 → Ag+ + NO3−, i=2. ΔTb = i × Kb × m = 2 × 0.5 × 0.1 = 0.1. So boiling point = 100 + 0.1 = 100.10°C.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of boiling point elevation and van't Hoff factor.

questions.question_text: The boiling point of water in a 0.1 molal silver nitrate solution (solution A) is x °C. To this solution A, an equal volume of 0.1 molal aqueous barium chloride solution is added to make a new solution B. The difference in the boiling points of water in the two solutions A and B is y × 10⁻² °C. (Assume: Densities of the solutions A and B are the same as that of water and the soluble salts dissociate completely. Use: Molal elevation constant Kb = 0.5 K kg mol⁻¹; Boiling point of pure water as 100°C.) The value of |y| is
questions.sub_topic: Colligative properties, Elevation of boiling point
questions.solution_summary: After mixing equal volumes: BaCl2 + 2AgNO3 → Ba(NO3)2 + 2AgCl. Final molality of Ba(NO3)2 = 0.05 m (i=3), AgNO3 left = 0.05 m (i=2). Total i×m = 3×0.05 + 2×0.05 = 0.15. ΔTb = 0.15×0.5 = 0.075. Difference = 0.1 - 0.075 = 0.025 = 2.5 × 10⁻². So y = 2.5.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires stoichiometric calculation after mixing and boiling point elevation.

questions.question_text: Given: [Image]. The compound(s), which on reaction with HNO3 will give the product having degree of rotation, [α]D = -52.7° is(are) [Options with images]
questions.solution_summary: All four compounds on nitration give product with specific rotation -52.7°.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of optical activity and nitration products.

questions.question_text: The reaction of Q with PhSNa yields an organic compound (major product) that gives positive Carius test on treatment with Na2O2 followed by addition of BaCl2. The correct option(s) for Q is(are) [Options with images]
questions.sub_topic: SNAr reaction, Carius test for sulphur
questions.solution_summary: In options (B) and (C), NO2 group (EWG) is not present ortho or para to the leaving group, so SNAr reaction will not be possible. Options A and D have activating nitro groups.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of SNAr mechanism and directing effects.

questions.question_text:
The correct statement(s) related to colloids is(are)
(A) The process of precipitating colloidal sol by an electrolyte is called peptization.
(B) Colloidal solution freezes at higher temperature than the true solution at the same concentration.
(C) Surfactants form micelle above critical micelle concentration (CMC). CMC depends on temperature.
(D) Micelles are macromolecular colloids.
questions.solution_summary: (A) is wrong – precipitation by electrolyte is coagulation, not peptization. (B) correct – molar mass of sol is higher, so colligative properties are smaller, hence freezing point of sol > true solution. (C) correct – micelles form above CMC and above Kraft temperature. (D) wrong – micelles are associated colloids, not macromolecular.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires recall of colloid properties.

questions.question_text: An ideal gas undergoes a reversible isothermal expansion from state I to state II followed by a reversible adiabatic expansion from state II to state III. The correct plot(s) representing the changes from state I to state III is(are) (p: pressure, V: volume, T: temperature, H: enthalpy, S: entropy) [Options with graphs]
questions.solution_summary: For isothermal expansion: T constant, H constant, S increases, pV decreases. For adiabatic expansion: S constant, T decreases, H decreases. The correct plots are A, B, D.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of thermodynamic process graphs.

questions.question_text:
The correct statement(s) related to the metal extraction processes is(are)
(A) A mixture of PbS and PbO undergoes self-reduction to produce Pb and SO2.
(B) In the extraction process of copper from copper pyrites, silica is added to produce copper silicate.
(C) Partial oxidation of sulphide ore of copper by roasting, followed by self-reduction produces blister copper.
(D) In cyanide process, zinc powder is utilized to precipitate gold from Na[Au(CN)2].
questions.sub_topic: Extraction of metals, Self-reduction, Cyanide process
questions.solution_summary: (A) PbS + 2PbO → 3Pb + SO2 (correct). (B) Silica removes FeO as slag, not copper silicate (wrong). (C) Cu2S + O2 → Cu2O + SO2; Cu2S + 2Cu2O → 6Cu + SO2 (self-reduction) – correct. (D) Zn + 2Na[Au(CN)2] → Na2[Zn(CN)4] + 2Au – correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of metallurgical processes.

questions.question_text:
A mixture of two salts is used to prepare a solution S, which gives the following results: [Results image]. The correct option(s) for the salt mixture is(are)
(A) Pb(NO3)2 and Zn(NO3)2
(B) Pb(NO3)2 and Bi(NO3)3
(C) AgNO3 and Bi(NO3)3
(D) Pb(NO3)2 and Hg(NO3)2
questions.solution_summary: Pb(OH)2, Zn(OH)2, Bi(OH)3 are white precipitates, but Hg(OH)2 is unstable. PbCl2 is white ppt. So options A, B, C are correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of qualitative analysis and precipitation.

questions.question_text: The maximum number of possible isomers (including stereoisomers) which may be formed on monobromination of 1-methylcyclohex-1-ene using Br2 and UV light is _____.
questions.solution_summary: Monobromination of 1-methylcyclohex-1-ene gives various positional and stereoisomers; total 13 isomers.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires counting of all possible isomers including stereoisomers.

questions.question_text: In the reaction given below, the total number of atoms having sp2 hybridization in the major product P is [Reaction scheme image]
questions.solution_summary: Counting the sp2 hybridised atoms in the major product P gives 12.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires counting of sp2 hybridised atoms in product structure.

questions.question_text: The total number of possible isomers for [Pt(NH3)4Cl2]Br2 is _____.
questions.solution_summary: The complex [Pt(NH3)4Cl2]Br2 can show geometrical isomerism (cis-trans) and ionisation isomerism (Br− as counter ion or ligand). Total isomers = 6.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires counting of geometrical and ionisation isomers.

questions.question_text: The reaction sequence(s) that would lead to o-xylene as the major product is(are) [Reaction scheme options]
questions.solution_summary: All given reaction sequences lead to o-xylene as the major product.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of synthesis routes to o-xylene.

questions.question_text:
Correct option(s) for the following sequence of reactions is (are) [Reaction scheme]
(A) Q = KNO2, W = LiAlH4
(B) R = benzenamine, V = KCN
(C) Q = AgNO2, R = phenylmethanamine
(D) W = LiAlH4, V = AgCN
questions.solution_summary: From the reaction sequence, Q is AgNO2 (for nitration via AgNO2?), R is phenylmethanamine (benzylamine), W is LiAlH4, V is AgCN. Options C and D are correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of reagents and products in multi-step synthesis.

questions.question_text:
For the following reaction: 2X + Y → P, the rate of reaction is d[P]/dt = k[X]. Two moles of X are mixed with one mole of Y to make 1.0 L of solution. At 50 s, 0.5 mole of Y is left in the reaction mixture. The correct statement(s) about the reaction is(are) (Use: ln 2 = 0.693)
(A) The rate constant, k, of the reaction is 13.86 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹
(B) Half-life of X is 50s
(C) At 50 s, -d[X]/dt = 13.86 × 10⁻³ mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹
(D) At 100 s, -d[Y]/dt = 3.46 × 10⁻³ mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹
questions.solution_summary: From stoichiometry, at t=50, X left = 1 mol, Y left = 0.5 mol. Rate = k[X] = (1/2)(-d[X]/dt). Since -d[X]/dt = 2k[X]. Half-life = ln2/(2k) = 50 s ⇒ k = 6.93×10⁻³. So A is wrong (k = 6.93×10⁻³, not 13.86×10⁻⁴). B correct. C: -d[X]/dt = 2k[X] = 13.86×10⁻³. D: -d[Y]/dt = k[X] = 6.93×10⁻³ × 0.5? Wait at 100s, X concentration? X = 2 - 2(1 - 0.5?) Actually at t=100, X left = 2 - 2×(1 - e^{-kt})? Better to trust solution: D is correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires derivation of rate laws and half-life from stoichiometry.

questions.question_text:
Some standard electrode potentials at 298 K are given below: Pb2+/Pb –0.13 V, Ni2+/Ni –0.24 V, Cd2+/Cd –0.40 V, Fe2+/Fe –0.44 V. To a solution containing 0.001 M of X2+ and 0.1 M of Y2+, the metal rods X and Y are inserted (at 298 K) and connected by a conducting wire. This resulted in dissolution of X. The correct combination(s) of X and Y, respectively, is(are) (Given: R=8.314, F=96500)
(A) Cd and Ni
(B) Cd and Fe
(C) Ni and Pb
(D) Ni and Fe
questions.solution_summary: For dissolution of X, X must be anode (oxidation). Calculate Ecell using Nernst equation for each combination. Options A, B, C give positive Ecell (spontaneous), D gives negative.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of Nernst equation to determine spontaneity.

questions.question_text:
The pair(s) of complexes wherein both exhibit tetrahedral geometry is(are) (Note: py = pyridine. Given: Atomic numbers: Fe=26, Co=27, Ni=28, Cu=29)
(A) [FeCl4]− and [Fe(CO)4]2−
(B) [Co(CO)4]− and [CoCl4]2−
(C) [Ni(CO)4] and [Ni(CN)4]2−
(D) [Cu(py)4]+ and [Cu(CN)4]3−
questions.solution_summary: For tetrahedral geometry: sp3 hybridisation. [FeCl4]− (Fe3+ d5, weak field, sp3), [Fe(CO)4]2− (Fe2+ d8? Actually CO strong field gives pairing, but [Fe(CO)4]2− is tetrahedral). [Co(CO)4]− and [CoCl4]2− are tetrahedral. [Ni(CO)4] is tetrahedral, but [Ni(CN)4]2− is square planar (Ni2+ d8, strong field). So C is wrong. [Cu(py)4]+ and [Cu(CN)4]3− both are tetrahedral (Cu+ d10, sp3). So A, B, D are correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires determination of geometry using VBT and ligand field.

questions.question_text:
The correct statement(s) related to oxoacids of phosphorous is(are)
(A) Upon heating, H3PO3 undergoes disproportionation reaction to produce H3PO4 and PH3.
(B) While H3PO3 can act as reducing agent, H3PO4 cannot.
(C) H3PO3 is a monobasic acid.
(D) The H atom of P-H bond in H3PO3 is not ionizable in water.
questions.solution_summary: H3PO3 on heating disproportionates: 4H3PO3 → 3H3PO4 + PH3 (A correct). H3PO3 has P-H bonds, so it can act as reducing agent; H3PO4 cannot (B correct). H3PO3 is dibasic (two OH groups), not monobasic (C wrong). P-H bonds are non-ionizable (D correct).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of oxoacids of phosphorus and their properties.

questions.question_text: At 298 K the limiting molar conductivity of a weak monobasic acid is 4×10² S cm² mol⁻¹. At 298 K for an aqueous solution of the acid the degree of dissociation is α and the molar conductivity is y×10² S cm² mol⁻¹. At 298 K upon 20 times dilution with water, the molar conductivity of the solution becomes 3y×10² S cm² mol⁻¹. The value of α is
questions.solution_summary: Let initial concentration be C. After 20 times dilution, concentration = C/20. α1 = Λm/Λ° = y/4 = α. α2 = (3y)/4 = 3α. For weak acid, Ka = Cα²/(1-α) = (C/20)(3α)²/(1-3α). Solving gives α = 0.22.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of Ostwald's dilution law and molar conductivity.

questions.question_text: At 298 K the limiting molar conductivity of a weak monobasic acid is 4×10² S cm² mol⁻¹. At 298 K for an aqueous solution of the acid the degree of dissociation is α and the molar conductivity is y×10² S cm² mol⁻¹. At 298 K upon 20 times dilution with water, the molar conductivity of the solution becomes 3y×10² S cm² mol⁻¹. The value of y is
questions.solution_summary: From previous, α = 0.22. y = 4α = 0.88.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Simple calculation from α.

questions.question_text: Reaction of x g of Sn with HCl quantitatively produced a salt. Entire amount of the salt reacted with y g of nitrobenzene in the presence of required amount of HCl to produce 1.29 g of an organic salt (quantitatively). (Use Molar masses: H=1, C=12, N=14, O=16, Cl=35, Sn=119). The value of x is
questions.solution_summary: Sn + 2HCl → SnCl2 + H2. SnCl2 reduces nitrobenzene to aniline (SnCl2 + nitrobenzene → anilinium chloride). Moles of Sn = x/119. Moles of anilinium chloride = x/119 (since 3 moles SnCl2 reduce 1 mole nitrobenzene? Actually from solution: Moles of anilinium chloride = x/357. Given mass of anilinium chloride = 1.29 g, molar mass = 129.5? The solution gives x = 3.57.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires stoichiometric calculation involving redox reaction.

questions.question_text: Reaction of x g of Sn with HCl quantitatively produced a salt. Entire amount of the salt reacted with y g of nitrobenzene in the presence of required amount of HCl to produce 1.29 g of an organic salt (quantitatively). (Use Molar masses: H=1, C=12, N=14, O=16, Cl=35, Sn=119). The value of y is
questions.solution_summary: From x=3.57, moles of Sn = 3.57/119 = 0.03 mol. Moles of nitrobenzene = 0.03/3 = 0.01 mol. Mass of nitrobenzene = 0.01 × 123 = 1.23 g.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires stoichiometric calculation.

questions.question_text: A sample (5.6 g) containing iron is completely dissolved in cold dilute HCl to prepare a 250 mL of solution. Titration of 25.0 mL of this solution requires 12.5 mL of 0.03 M KMnO4 solution to reach the end point. Number of moles of Fe2+ present in 250 mL solution is x × 10⁻² (consider complete dissolution of FeCl2). The value of x is
questions.solution_summary: meq of Fe2+ = meq of KMnO4. n_Fe2+ × 1000 × 1 = 12.5 × 0.03 × 5 × 10 (since 25 mL aliquot from 250 mL). So n_Fe2+ = 1.875 × 10⁻² mol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of redox titration stoichiometry.

questions.question_text: A sample (5.6 g) containing iron is completely dissolved in cold dilute HCl to prepare a 250 mL of solution. Titration of 25.0 mL of this solution requires 12.5 mL of 0.03 M KMnO4 solution to reach the end point. The amount of iron present in the sample is y% by weight. The value of y is

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires calculation of percentage composition from titration data.

questions.question_text:
The amount of energy required to break a bond is same as the amount of energy released when the same bond is formed. In gaseous state, the energy required for homolytic cleavage of a bond is called Bond Dissociation Energy (BDE) or Bond Strength. BDE is affected by s-character of the bond and the stability of the radicals formed. Shorter bonds are typically stronger bonds. BDEs for some bonds are given. Correct match of the C-H bonds (shown in bold) in Column J with their BDE in Column K is [Table]
(A) P-iii, Q-iv, R-ii, S-i
(B) P-ii, Q-ii, R-iii, S-iv
(C) P-iii, Q-ii, R-ii, S-iv
(D) P-ii, Q-ii, R-iv, S-iii
questions.solution_summary: As s-character increases, BDE increases. Order: H-CH(CH3)2 (sp3, 95), H-CH2Ph (benzylic, 88? Actually given: P-iii (95), Q-iv (88), R-ii (110), S-i (132) – match accordingly.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of BDE trends.

questions.question_text:
For the following reaction: CH4(g) + Cl2(g) → CH3Cl(g) + HCl(g) (light). The correct statement is
(A) Initiation step is exothermic with ΔH° = –58 kcal mol⁻¹
(B) Propagation step involving CH3 formation is exothermic with ΔH° = –2 kcal mol⁻¹
(C) Propagation step involving CH3Cl formation is endothermic with ΔH° = +27 kcal mol⁻¹
(D) The reaction is exothermic with ΔH° = –25 kcal mol⁻¹
questions.solution_summary: Using bond enthalpies: CH4 + Cl2 → CH3Cl + HCl. ΔH = (BDE C-H + BDE Cl-Cl) - (BDE C-Cl + BDE H-Cl) = 105 + 58 - 85 - 103 = -25 kcal/mol. The reaction is exothermic with ΔH = -25 kcal/mol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires calculation of enthalpy using bond dissociation energies.

questions.question_text:
The reaction of K3[Fe(CN)6] with freshly prepared FeSO4 solution produces a dark blue precipitate called Turnbull's blue. Reaction of K4[Fe(CN)6] with the FeSO4 solution in complete absence of air produces a white precipitate X which turns blue in air. Mixing the FeSO4 solution with NaNO3 followed by a slow addition of concentrated H2SO4 through the side of the test tube produces a brown ring. Precipitate X is
(A) Fe4[Fe(CN)6]3
(B) K2Fe[Fe(CN)6]
(C) Fe2[Fe(CN)6]
(D) K4[Fe(CN)6]·FeSO4
questions.solution_summary: K4[Fe(CN)6] + FeSO4 → K2Fe[Fe(CN)6] (white precipitate). On exposure to air, it oxidizes to blue.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of cyanide complexes and their reactions.

questions.question_text:
The reaction of K3[Fe(CN)6] with freshly prepared FeSO4 solution produces a dark blue precipitate called Turnbull's blue. Reaction of K4[Fe(CN)6] with the FeSO4 solution in complete absence of air produces a white precipitate X which turns blue in air. Mixing the FeSO4 solution with NaNO3 followed by a slow addition of concentrated H2SO4 through the side of the test tube produces a brown ring. Among the following, the brown ring is due to the formation of
(A) [Fe(NO2)2(SO4)2]2−
(B) [Fe(NO)2(SO4)2]
(C) [Fe(NO)2(SO4)2]2−
(D) [Fe(H2O)5NO]2+
questions.solution_summary: The brown ring complex is [Fe(H2O)5NO]2+ (nitrosyl ferrous complex).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of brown ring complex.

questions.question_text: One mole of an ideal gas at 900K undergoes two reversible processes, I followed by II, as shown below. If the work done by the gas in the two process are same, the value of ln(V3/V2) [Given: molar heat capacity at constant volume, Cv,m = 5/2 R]
questions.sub_topic: Adiabatic and isothermal processes, Work done
questions.solution_summary: Process I is adiabatic (entropy constant). Work done = ΔU = nCvΔT = (5/2)R(450-900) = -1125R? Actually solution: W1 = -1800R (from ΔU = 450R - 2250R = -1800R). Process II is isothermal (U constant). W2 = -nRT ln(V3/V2). Given W1 = W2 and T=180K (from U = nCvT, 450R = (5/2)RT ⇒ T=180). So -1800R = -R×180 ln(V3/V2) ⇒ ln(V3/V2) = 10.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of thermodynamics to adiabatic and isothermal processes.

questions.question_text: Consider a helium (He) atom that absorbs a photon of wavelength 330 nm. The change in the velocity (in cm s⁻¹) of He atom after the photon absorption is (Assume: Momentum is conserved when photon is absorbed. Use: Planck constant = 6.6×10⁻³⁴ J s, Avogadro number = 6×10²³ mol⁻¹, Molar mass of He = 4 g mol⁻¹)

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires application of de Broglie wavelength and momentum conservation.

questions.question_text: Ozonolysis of ClO2 produces an oxide of chlorine. The average oxidation state of chlorine in this oxide is
questions.solution_summary: 2ClO2 + 2O3 → Cl2O6 + 2O2. In Cl2O6, average oxidation state of Cl is +6.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of chlorine oxides and oxidation state calculation.
